{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27456","id":2543361873,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86XmJ9R","number":27456,"title":"ENH: Discussion: Integrating Numpy with CPython interpreter specializations","user":{"login":"mdboom","id":38294,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM4Mjk0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/38294?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom","html_url":"https://github.com/mdboom","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdboom/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-09-23T18:32:26Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T17:40:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

There is an interesting recent paper about exposing the bytecode interpreter specialization features added in Python 3.11 (aka quickening) to arbitrary extension types.
 
https://drops.dagstuhl.de/entities/document/10.4230/LIPIcs.ECOOP.2024.6

For background, the bytecode specialization work for Python 3.11 essentially amounts to: if we notice that a generic instruction `BINARY_OP_ADD` is being called from a hot loop, replace it with an instruction that is specialized for the specific types that are present at the call site, e.g. `BINARY_OP_ADD_INT`.  This concept is the bulk of what gave Python 3.11 its ~30% speed improvement.

The innovation in the paper is to expose this facility to arbitrary extension types, so that a type can cache the results of dealing with dynamism at each call site and then skip that work on subsequent calls.  In the case of Numpy, this is all the "work planning" of handling casting, broadcasting, overrided ufuncs etc. that *usually* (but not always) doesn't change between calls at the same call site.  A key finding of the paper is that this overhead is enough, especially in the case of small to medium sized arrays, that the performance improvements can be very significant, on the order of more than 2x.

Prototype patches against CPython and Numpy used for the paper are available here:

Changes to Numpy: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-numpy-ae
Changes to CPython: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-ae

As a member of the Faster CPython team, I'd like to work on making this a reality, but the majority of the work actually needs to happen in Numpy, and I could use some help on that side and would also like to gauge the interest of the Numpy developers first.  (I've made modest contributions to Numpy in the past, but I definitely no longer have my bearings).  Looking at the patch to Numpy, you can see it involves generating code to handle and specialize many of the cases.  The authors used their own mako templates to do this, but they admit in the paper that it would perhaps be better to leverage the code generation that exists already.

What do you all think is the best way to proceed?  Would this require a NEP?

(For reference some [earlier discussion in the faster-cpython/ideas tracker](https://github.com/faster-cpython/ideas/issues/696)).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2371917472","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27456#issuecomment-2371917472","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456","id":2371917472,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86NYJag","user":{"login":"fberlakovich","id":7955528,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5NTU1Mjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7955528?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich","html_url":"https://github.com/fberlakovich","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-24T17:40:28Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T17:40:28Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Hi! 
Glad you liked the paper and that you are experimenting with it. I agree with @eendebakpt that NumPy probably also has internal room for optimization (the ufunc lookup machinery is quite complex), but I think most optimizations would need some sort of link to a specific occurence in the program to make assumptions about their context (e.g. the other side is always an int). Please let me know if you have any questions. I hope I will find time soon to repair the docker image to ease reproduction of the results.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2371917472/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

There is an interesting recent paper about exposing the bytecode interpreter specialization features added in Python 3.11 (aka quickening) to arbitrary extension types.
 
https://drops.dagstuhl.de/entities/document/10.4230/LIPIcs.ECOOP.2024.6

For background, the bytecode specialization work for Python 3.11 essentially amounts to: if we notice that a generic instruction `BINARY_OP_ADD` is being called from a hot loop, replace it with an instruction that is specialized for the specific types that are present at the call site, e.g. `BINARY_OP_ADD_INT`.  This concept is the bulk of what gave Python 3.11 its ~30% speed improvement.

The innovation in the paper is to expose this facility to arbitrary extension types, so that a type can cache the results of dealing with dynamism at each call site and then skip that work on subsequent calls.  In the case of Numpy, this is all the "work planning" of handling casting, broadcasting, overrided ufuncs etc. that *usually* (but not always) doesn't change between calls at the same call site.  A key finding of the paper is that this overhead is enough, especially in the case of small to medium sized arrays, that the performance improvements can be very significant, on the order of more than 2x.

Prototype patches against CPython and Numpy used for the paper are available here:

Changes to Numpy: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-numpy-ae
Changes to CPython: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-ae

As a member of the Faster CPython team, I'd like to work on making this a reality, but the majority of the work actually needs to happen in Numpy, and I could use some help on that side and would also like to gauge the interest of the Numpy developers first.  (I've made modest contributions to Numpy in the past, but I definitely no longer have my bearings).  Looking at the patch to Numpy, you can see it involves generating code to handle and specialize many of the cases.  The authors used their own mako templates to do this, but they admit in the paper that it would perhaps be better to leverage the code generation that exists already.

What do you all think is the best way to proceed?  Would this require a NEP?

(For reference some [earlier discussion in the faster-cpython/ideas tracker](https://github.com/faster-cpython/ideas/issues/696)).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2382698262","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27456#issuecomment-2382698262","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27456","id":2382698262,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86OBRcW","user":{"login":"fberlakovich","id":7955528,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5NTU1Mjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7955528?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich","html_url":"https://github.com/fberlakovich","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fberlakovich/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-30T10:12:03Z","updated_at":"2024-09-30T10:12:03Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> I suspect that the single most important question to assess the benefits here is what "small to medium sized" means. The results in the paper don't say at all as far as I can tell. If there's code in working state, producing some graphs like this (from [this notebook](https://github.com/rgommers/explore-array-computing/blob/master/explore_xnd.ipynb)) for benchmarks as a function of input array size for benchmark problems that show larger speedups would be very helpful:

I agree. For the paper we simply used the `paper` input sizes from the NPBench paper, but more detailed measurements would be interesting. Generally, a component analysis to figure out the impact of each optimization would be interesting, but we simply couldn't fit it in anymore before the paper deadline.

> * A large chunk of the ufunc improvement may be the result cache?  I am a bit worried that such a cache should not be persistent forever because it could waste memory indefinitely?  OTOH, I could imagine that the existing data cache should be improved (and maybe extended with an array free-list or some other optimization).

As with basically the entire implementation, there is almost certainly room for improvement, it is very researchy ;). There is, however, a simple cache invalidation heuristic that clears large cached arrays at the end of a function: https://github.com/fberlakovich/cmq-numpy-ae/blob/master/numpy/core/src/multiarray/number.c#L412. 

> 2\. I do really like the indexing specialization example quite a lot and similarly, I wonder/assume that we can specialize things like `arr + 1` to `arr + np.array(1.0)` (caching the second one).
>     Both are cases that add very significant overheads to operations (if the result is a small array or scalar).

I also like this one conceptually, but iirc the benefit compared to the other optimizations was low. I gathered this only from playing with the benchmarks, however. Only a sound component-wise analysis can tell for sure.

> * If this could be automatically found (easily!) the story and use-case for such fused ufuncs would be even more compelling and in that case provide significant speed-ups to large arrays (rather than small to midsized ones as probably the case currently).

Yes, that would be super cool. I also talked to @omelancon about possible benefits of combining our work (he had an excellent talk on static basic block versioning for Python, just before me).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2382698262/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
